executive_summary: Fri placed 11 orders (vs 13.0 avg, -15.4%), $514.26 ex. HST order value (-26.1%). Cost per new customer $34.99 — better than $40.14 7d avg but still 40% above the $25 ceiling. Zero second-order customers. Deliveries at 11 for $617.89 ex. HST (-19.1%).
analyst_brief:
**The cost-per-new-customer problem is structural, not daily noise.** Friday's $34.99 per new customer is actually the best single day in the trailing week — the 7-day average is $40.14. Over the full 7 days, Tumbil spent $722.48 on ads and acquired 18 new customers. To get that 7-day average down to the $25 ceiling, you'd need roughly 29 new customers on the same spend — 60% more than you're getting. The spend itself is steady (~$103/day), so this is a conversion problem, not a budget problem. The ads are generating a consistent ~70 clicks per day; only about 2-3 of those turn into first-time orders.

The reorder cohort data adds some comfort here: recent cohorts are converting at 28-41% within 21 days, so the customers you do acquire tend to stick. But that doesn't fix the front of the pipeline. The April 13 cohort (41.2% reorder rate) is the high-water mark in recent months — if the quality of new customers is staying that high, the real lever is getting more of them through the door at a reasonable cost.

**Zero second-order customers on Friday is worth flagging.** The 7-day average is 2.0 per day, and there have been two other zero days this week (May 17 and May 20). That's 3 out of 7 days with no one coming back for a second order. This could be random variation on small numbers, but it's also the segment that determines whether your acquisition spend pays off — a new customer who never returns is a $35 loss. Worth watching over the next week to see if the second-order rate recovers.

**The cancellation rate keeps creeping up.** The rolling 7-day rate is now 5.5%, up 0.3 percentage points from yesterday's reading of 5.2%. The 30-day baseline is 3.1%. Five cancellations in the last 7 days vs 12 in the prior 30 — the recent week is running at nearly double the monthly pace. None of yesterday's 11 orders were cancelled, so the elevated rate is from earlier in the week. Still, this is tracking in the wrong direction and worth investigating — are specific cities, WashPros, or order types driving it?

**WashPro quality alert: WashPro 2323 has 3 low ratings in the last 30 days, averaging 3.0 stars.** Friday also saw a new 3-star rating for WashPro 2506 (order 5645). With overall ratings dipping to 4.71 yesterday vs the 4.80 7d average (1 low rating out of 14), service quality is holding broadly but these individual WashPros need attention. WashPro 2323 in particular — three low ratings is a pattern, not a bad day.

**On organic search:** all 42 monitored pages are indexed, no flags. The Kitchener city page stands out — 371 impressions over the last 7 days with zero clicks, averaging position 7.8. That's Google showing the page frequently but no one finding it worth clicking. Since the city page meta title and description are template-level (shared across all 24 city pages), a template-level title/description tweak would affect all cities — but the Kitchener data suggests the current template isn't compelling enough when the city name alone isn't a draw. Hamilton has a similar pattern: 420 impressions, 4 clicks, position 12.8. These two pages alone represent nearly 800 impressions per week with almost no traffic.

**The one thing today:** The acquisition cost problem won't fix itself at $103/day spend producing 2.6 new customers. Before adding budget or changing bids, it's worth auditing which campaigns and search terms actually produce first-time orders (not just registrations — the Google Ads conversion count of 7 yesterday includes registrations, not purchases). Friday's search terms show spend on "calgary laundromat" ($4.30, 0 conversions), "cleaning soiled clothes" ($2.51, 0 conversions), and other terms unlikely to produce pickup-and-delivery customers. The Calgary campaign spent $13.65 with zero conversions. Understanding where the 3 actual new customers came from — and whether some campaigns are burning budget on the wrong audience — is the highest-leverage 30-minute exercise.

days.analyst_brief:
**Wednesday was a regulars day.** Eight of 10 placed orders came from customers who've ordered three or more times. Only 1 new customer showed up — the lowest in the 7-day window and well below the 2.4 daily average. One second-order customer rounded it out.

That's not alarming on its own — single-day new customer counts bounce around a lot at this volume. But it's worth watching. Looking at the week, the daily new customer numbers have been: 3, 3, 2, 1, 5, 2, 1. Sunday (May 4) spiked to 5 with the highest spend day ($148.18), and Wednesday came in at just 1 on lower spend ($105.56). There's no clear trend yet — it's noisy — but two of the last three days landed at 1-2 new customers.

**The delivered side was soft but not unusual.** Eight deliveries for $426.50 ex. HST. That's -23.4% vs the 7d average, but the average is inflated by Monday's 16-delivery day ($884.88 ex. HST). Delivered AOV was solid at $53.31 ex. HST, above the 7d average.

**Cost per new customer is in excellent shape.** At $10.56 per placed order against $105.56 in ad spend, you're well under the $25 ceiling. The 7d average is $10.36 — consistent and healthy. Even if you count only the 1 new customer, you spent $105.56 to acquire them, which is high for a single day — but that's not the right way to read it, since regulars also come through paid search. The blended number is what matters and it's strong.

**Search terms worth noting.** The top three search terms by spend each cost $10-14 for a single click with no conversion: "laundry pick up and delivery near me" ($13.72), "laundry pickup and delivery service near me" ($11.02), and "pick up laundry service toronto" ($10.41). These are high-intent queries but the click costs are steep and none converted on Wednesday. This is a normal variance day, not necessarily a problem — but if the pattern repeats across several days, it'd be worth reviewing whether the landing pages match the search intent.

"Poplin laundry service" appeared ($3.09, 1 click, no conversion) — someone searching for a competitor and seeing Tumbil. These searches for other brands that still convert are valuable when they do convert; worth keeping an eye on this one.

**On the organic side**, "laundry service barrie" is sitting at position 11.3 with 31 impressions and 2 clicks — right on the edge of page 1. The Barrie city page is at position 8.3 with 157 impressions and 4 clicks. Barrie is pulling decent organic interest. The Google Ads campaign for Barrie spent $4.62 with 0 conversions — organic may be the better channel there.

**The 7-day cancellation rate is 2.6%, unchanged day-over-day and well below the 30-day baseline of 4.6%.** Zero failed payments. Service quality looks excellent — 14 ratings yesterday, all 5 stars, and only 1 low rating (a 3-star) in the entire 7-day window. The rating response rate of 93.4% is unusually high, which means you're getting a nearly complete picture of customer satisfaction.

**Reorder trends**: The most recent complete cohort (week of Apr 13) had 9 new customers and 4 reordered within 21 days — a 44.4% reorder rate. That's the second-best reading in the 8-week window, behind only the Feb 16 cohort (50%). Small sample, but the direction is positive.

**Bottom line for today**: Nothing is broken. The business ran on its regulars yesterday, which is what you want — 80% of orders from people who keep coming back. The worry would be if new customer acquisition stays at 1/day for multiple days running. Tomorrow's number will tell you if Wednesday was just noise or the start of a dip.

days.analyst_brief:
**The new-customer pipeline nearly shut off on Thursday.** Only 1 new customer placed an order, versus the 7-day average of 2.1. That single acquisition absorbed the full $122.92 of ad spend, pushing cost per new customer to nearly 5x the $25 ceiling. This is a noisy metric on low-volume days — the 7-day average is $55.73, which is already over the ceiling — but Thursday was especially stark.

The good news: the business didn't fall apart. Regulars placed 7 orders and 2 second-timers came back, so placed volume held at 10 (only 12% below average). Average order size was $61.13 ex. HST, the highest single day this week, pulled up by the regulars ordering bigger loads. Total placed order value was $611.27 ex. HST, nearly flat to the $627.19 average. The regulars are doing their job.

**Delivered orders were genuinely weak at 7** (vs 10.7 avg), and revenue followed at $348.88 ex. HST (vs $546.84 avg). That's a 36% miss. Deliveries are a lagging indicator — these are orders that were placed days ago — so Thursday's placed performance won't show up in delivery numbers for a bit. Still, the delivery dip has been building: Monday was 16, then 8, now 7. Worth watching whether this is just timing or something operational.

**On the ad side, one search term deserves a closer look.** "Simple laundry" cost $30.58 on a single click with no conversion. That's a competitor name eating a quarter of the day's spend in one shot. I'm flagging it for manual review — not recommending any keyword changes, just making sure you see it. The rest of the search terms were typical city and service queries. "Laundry service near me" drove 2 conversions across its appearances, which is solid.

**The 7-day cost per new customer at $55.73 is the more concerning trend.** It's been above the $25 ceiling consistently. The week acquired 15 new customers on $836 of total spend. That math ($55.73 per new customer) only works if the customers who come back bring the blended cost down — and the reorder data is encouraging there. The April 13 cohort (the most recent with full 21-day data) had a 45.5% reorder rate, the best since mid-February. So people who sign up are coming back at a healthy rate; the issue is the front of the pipeline, not what happens after.

**Cancellations continue improving.** The rolling 7-day rate dropped to 1.2% (down 1.4 percentage points vs yesterday's reading of the same metric), well below the 30-day baseline of 4.3%. Zero failed payments. Ratings remain excellent at 4.9 average, with 9 of 10 ratings at 5 stars and zero low ratings.

**On search visibility:** all 42 monitored pages are indexed, no flags. Ottawa's city page gets the most impressions of any city page (493) but only a 1% click-through rate — the page ranks around position 8.7, so it's visible but the listing isn't compelling enough to click. Hamilton is similar: 432 impressions, 0.93% click-through, position 8.1. Since the meta titles and descriptions are template-level across all 24 city pages, any improvement there would lift all of them. Mississauga (331 impressions, 0.3% click-through) would benefit the most from better meta copy on a per-impression basis.

**Bottom line:** Thursday was a regulars-carry-the-load day. The business is healthy operationally (great ratings, low cancellations, strong reorder rates). The weakness is at the top — not enough new people are finding Tumbil and converting, and when they do, the cost is too high. The "simple laundry" spend leak is worth investigating manually, and the city page meta descriptions are a low-effort opportunity to improve organic click-through across the board.

days.analyst_brief:
**Friday was a regulars-only day.** Eight of nine orders came from people who've ordered three or more times. One new customer. Zero second-orders. That's the entire story.

The cost per new customer number — $107.79 — looks alarming, but it's mostly a small-denominator problem. You spent $107.79 on ads and got 1 new customer instead of the usual 2. On a per-order basis (blending new and returning), each order cost $11.98 in ad spend, which is actually below the 7-day average of $12.43. The ads drove traffic (62 clicks), but that traffic mostly didn't convert to *new* accounts.

This is the third day in a row with only 1 new customer. The 7-day average has drifted down to 1.9/day. For context, Sunday May 4 pulled in 5 new customers, which is propping up that average — strip that day out and the other six days averaged 1.3. The front of the pipeline is thinning.

**Where the ad money went.** "Laundry service toronto" ate $32.70 for 2 clicks and zero conversions. "Toronto wash and fold laundry service" cost $8.13 for 1 click, no conversion. These broad Toronto terms are expensive because they're competitive, and on days like Friday they can burn budget without producing a single new customer. The one search term that did convert — "wash and fold laundry service near me" — cost $2.44. Worth watching whether the high-intent "near me" queries consistently outperform the city-name queries in conversion.

**The good news: regulars are reliable and happy.** 8 orders from regulars, average order size of $58.00 ex. HST (above the $45 target), and a perfect 5.0 rating from 9 reviews. The 7-day cancellation rate is 1.4%, well below the 30-day baseline of 4.1%. The service itself is in great shape.

**The worry is the second-order gap.** Zero customers came back for their second order on Friday. The 7-day average is 1.3/day, but it's been 0 twice this week (Friday and Saturday). This is where customers either become regulars or disappear. The 21-day reorder data shows the most recent measurable cohort (week of Apr 13) came back at 40%, which is solid. But the daily second-order count is worth watching — if it stays at 0-1 for another week, that 40% cohort rate isn't translating to volume because there aren't enough new customers feeding in.

The same pattern played out last Sunday (May 11: 14 placed, but only 3 new).

**The second-order number (1 vs 1.7 avg) deserves watching, not action.** On any given day with low volume, second-order customers (people placing their second order ever) can easily be 0 or 1 by chance alone. The more meaningful signal is the weekly reorder cohort data: the week of April 13 hit 41.2% of new customers coming back within 21 days — the highest in the dataset. But the following week (April 20) dropped to 28.0%. It's too early to know which is the trend. We'll have clarity when the April 27 and May 4 cohorts mature in the next couple of weeks.

**A note on ad spend waste.** A few search terms yesterday are burning budget on things Tumbil doesn't do or can't serve: "dry cleaner" ($1.31), "iron services near me" ($1.35), "puffer jacket cleaning service near me" ($1.40), and "lavado" ($1.34 — this is Spanish for laundry; unlikely to convert in Ontario). That's about $5.40 in clear waste out of $83.33, or ~6.5%. Not enormous, but this is a flag-for-manual-review item, not something to act on in the daily report. These terms suggest the broad-match campaigns are occasionally catching queries outside Tumbil's service scope.

**Organic search: Kitchener is getting seen but not clicked.** The Kitchener city page had 305 impressions with 0 clicks at an average position of 6.3 over the past week. For context, that's more impressions than Hamilton (529, but Hamilton sits at position 13.2 and got 5 clicks). Position 6.3 means Kitchener is solidly on page 1, but the listing isn't compelling enough for anyone to tap. Since city page meta titles and descriptions are template-driven across all 24 cities, this isn't a Kitchener-specific fix — it's worth evaluating whether the template formula is doing its job across the board. Barrie is similar: 141 impressions, 0 clicks, position 11.

All 42 monitored pages remain indexed. No flags.

**Ratings remain excellent.** 4.93 average yesterday from 14 ratings, zero low scores. The 7-day response rate is 92.4% — customers are consistently rating, and the scores are consistently high. No WashPro alerts in the last 30 days.

**The one thing today:** The rolling cost per new customer ($34.67) is the biggest number that's off-target. Yesterday's $20.83 was a step in the right direction. Keep ad spend disciplined on days when impressions and clicks are naturally lower (Sundays), and let the rolling average come down. No need to change campaigns — the lower Sunday spend naturally produced better efficiency.

days.executive_summary: Mon placed orders steady at 15 (vs 14.7 avg, +2.0%). Delivered revenue strong at $1,077.49 ex. HST (+21.8%). Cost per new customer hit $33.10 — well above the $25 ceiling — on 3 new customers. WashPro 2323 flagged with 3 low ratings in 30 days.
days.analyst_brief:
**The cost-per-new-customer problem is persistent, not a one-day blip.**

Monday's $33.10 cost per new customer is above the $25 ceiling, but the more important number is the 7-day average: $34.19. That means you've been consistently spending ~$34 to acquire each new customer for the past week. Yesterday's $99.31 in ad spend produced 3 new customers. The 7-day average is 3.1 new customers per day at $107.47 in daily spend. The math: you're paying about a third more per acquisition than the business can sustain.

This isn't a "bad day" story — it's a trend that needs a decision. Either the ads need to convert better, or spend needs to come down, or the $25 target needs revisiting given current market conditions. The 7-day average is the honest signal here, and it's been running 37% above target.

**The delivered revenue number is genuinely strong — and it's worth understanding why.**

$1,077.49 ex. HST on 16 deliveries is +21.8% above the 7-day average. That $67.34 delivered average order size is the highest in the past week (the 7-day average is $57.89). This wasn't driven by more orders — it was driven by bigger orders. And looking at the customer mix on the placed side: 9 of 15 orders came from regulars (60%), 3 from people placing their second order, and 3 from brand-new customers. The regulars tend to order larger loads, so a day where they make up the majority pulls the average order size up.

The 3 second-order customers are a bright spot. The 7-day average is 1.9, so this is well above normal. Every one of those people is a step closer to becoming a regular. The cohort reorder data supports this: the week-of-April-13 group came back at 41.2% within 21 days, the best mature cohort in months. Whatever the experience is like right now, it's working for retention.

**Two low ratings yesterday — and one WashPro needs attention.**

The average in-app rating dipped to 4.64 (vs 4.86 over 7 days). Two 3-star ratings came in. One was for WashPro 2323, who now has **3 low ratings in the past 30 days** with an average of 3.0. That's the only WashPro flagged at the alert threshold. The other low rating was for WashPro 1641 (order 5303). Worth checking both orders, but 2323 is the pattern that needs a conversation.

**Search and indexing are clean — one opportunity buried in the data.**

All 42 monitored pages are indexed. No flags. On the organic side, the Kitchener city page is interesting: 242 impressions, average position 6.4, but zero clicks. That's a page sitting on page 1 of Google results that nobody clicks on. The meta title and description are template-driven across all 24 city pages, so this isn't a Kitchener-only fix — but it does suggest the template formula isn't compelling enough to earn clicks even when Google is showing the page. Ottawa is similar: 485 impressions, position 11.1, only 2 clicks (0.41% CTR). Hamilton: 446 impressions, position 13.7, 4 clicks (0.9%). These pages get seen. They just don't get clicked.

**The one thing:** The acquisition cost overshoot is the most consequential number in today's data. At $34.19 per new customer over 7 days vs a $25 ceiling, you're burning ~$9 extra per acquisition. With ~3 new customers a day, that's roughly $27/day in overspend — small in absolute terms, but it compounds, and it means the ad channel isn't currently paying for itself on a per-acquisition basis. Worth a focused look at which campaigns and search terms are producing actual orders (not just clicks) to see where the spend is going.

days.executive_summary: Tue placed 17 orders ($830.03 ex. HST), +11.1% vs 7d avg — strong day driven by 12 regulars. Deliveries lagged at 11 ($609.75 ex. HST, -27.5%). days.analyst_brief:
Tuesday's headline is a split personality: the front of the order pipeline was healthy (17 placed, above average) while the back end lagged (only 11 delivered, -27.5% vs the 7d average of $840.46 ex. HST). That delivery gap is almost certainly timing — orders placed Tuesday don't all get delivered Tuesday — so don't read the revenue line as a demand problem. Watch whether those orders show up in Wednesday/Thursday deliveries.

**The regulars are carrying the business right now.** 12 of 17 placed orders came from customers on their third-or-more order (71% of the day, vs 66% 7d average). That's good and bad: good because these people keep coming back, bad because the business isn't adding enough new customers to grow. Four new customers yesterday is slightly above the 3.4 daily average, but only slightly.

**Cost per new customer: $27.55 — above the $25 ceiling, but improving.** The 7d rolling average is $30.49, so Tuesday was actually a better-than-recent day. The math: $110.19 ad spend ÷ 4 new customers = $27.55. To get under $25 at this spend level, you'd need 5 new customers per day. The gap is one extra acquisition per day.

**Second-order customers dropped to 1 (vs 1.7 avg, -41%).** This is the retention metric that matters most right now. These are people who tried Tumbil once and came back for a second time — the signal that they're going to stick. The recent reorder cohort data shows the same pattern: the groups of customers who signed up in late April are coming back at about 28%, down from the 38-41% rate in late March/early April. Something changed in the first-order experience or follow-up for newer customers. Worth investigating whether WashPro quality, delivery timing, or follow-up messaging shifted in April.

**Cancellation rate jumped.** The rolling 7d rate is 3.7%, up 1.8 percentage points from yesterday's reading of 1.9% (the 30d baseline is 3.5%, so 3.7% is close to normal). Two orders were cancelled Tuesday specifically. Not alarming yet, but if another 2+ cancel today, the rolling rate will keep climbing.

**WashPro 2323 needs attention.** This WashPro has 3 low ratings in the last 30 days (average rating: 3.0). Yesterday's sole low rating — a 1-star on order #5526 — came from WashPro 4529, not 2323, but the pattern on 2323 is the more concerning one. Three complaints in a month from the same WashPro suggests a consistent quality issue, not a one-off bad day.

**SEO observation: Kitchener's city page got 331 impressions and zero clicks** (average position 6.7 — first page of Google). Waterloo: 132 impressions, zero clicks, position 7.9. These pages are being shown to searchers and nobody is clicking. Since the meta title and description are template-driven across all 24 city pages, this isn't a Kitchener-specific fix — but it's worth looking at what the template generates for these pages and whether the title/description actually tell a searcher why they'd click. Ottawa is similar: 471 impressions, 2 clicks, 0.42% click-through rate. The city pages collectively are getting thousands of impressions but converting almost none of them to site visits.

**The one thing today:** Dig into why the late-April new-customer cohorts are coming back at 28% instead of the 33-41% rate from March. If the answer is WashPro quality (2323's pattern), that's a conversation. If it's something else — slower delivery, different cities, a messaging gap — that's where the next order growth comes from. Getting second-order rate back to 35%+ on the ~25 new customers you acquire per week would mean 2-3 extra orders per week from people who are already in the system.

days.executive_summary: Wed placed orders dropped to 7 (vs 13.6 avg, -48.5%), dragging order value to $326.50 ex. HST (-56.7%). Cost per new customer hit $63.02 on just 2 acquisitions — 2.5x the $25 ceiling. Deliveries solid at 17 ($1,037.27 ex. HST, +13.1%).
days.analyst_brief:
Wednesday was the weakest placement day in the 7-day window — only 7 orders placed, against an average of 13.6. That's not a record low (Saturday the 17th also hit 11), but the combination of low volume and high ad spend makes it sting. You spent $126.04 on ads and got 2 new customers out of it, putting the cost per new customer at $63.02 — more than double the $25 ceiling and well above the 7-day rolling average of $34.48.

The delivery side tells a different story. 17 orders delivered at $1,037.27 ex. HST is the best revenue day in the past week. That's the pipeline from prior days flushing through, not a signal about Wednesday's demand.

**What's behind the 7-order day.** Looking at the week, placement volume has been lumpy: 11, 16, 18, 11, 15, 17, then 7. Wednesday didn't continue a trend — it broke one. Monday and Tuesday were actually strong (15 and 17 placed). The drop is sharp enough that it's worth asking whether something went wrong operationally (app issues, coverage gaps) or whether this is just a noisy midweek dip. Without GA4 session data, there's no way to tell whether fewer people showed up or whether the same number showed up and didn't convert.

**The acquisition math.** Two new customers on a $126 spend day is not a pattern you can sustain. But one day doesn't make a pattern — the 7-day cost per new customer is $34.48 (22 new customers on $758.57 total spend), which is still above the $25 ceiling but not alarmingly so. The real question is whether acquisition efficiency is trending the wrong way. Looking at the daily new-customer counts this week — 2, 7, 3, 2, 2, 4, 2 — only Thursday's 7 was clearly strong. The other six days averaged 2.5 new customers each. That's thin.

**Ad spend composition.** The GTA Click campaign took $64.77 (half the budget) with 44 clicks and zero tracked conversions. Google Ads conversion tracking still counts registrations, not purchases, so the 0.0 conversions number isn't alarming on its own — but it does mean you're flying blind on which campaigns actually produce orders. The city campaigns are small individually ($1–$10 each) but collectively add up. Some search terms are clearly off-target: "washer dryer combo rental" ($3.54, 2 clicks), "coin laundry near me" ($4.04, 2 clicks), "steam press near me" ($1.85) — these are people looking for physical laundromats or appliance rentals, not pickup-and-delivery. Worth flagging for manual review, but not something to act on without operator approval.

**The customer mix.** Five of Wednesday's 7 orders came from regulars. Zero second-order customers. That's concerning if it becomes a pattern — you need people coming back for a second order to build the habit loop. The week-of-Apr-27 cohort (the most recent with enough time to measure) shows a 25% reorder rate within 21 days, down from 41% for the week of Apr 13. Small sample sizes (8 vs 17 new customers) make this noisy, but it's the second week in a row below 30%. Worth watching.

**One bright spot: service quality.** Perfect 5.0 average rating yesterday across 11 reviews. The 7-day average is 4.81, and the response rate is 76% — customers are reviewing and they're happy. WashPro 2323 has three low ratings in the past 30 days (average 3.0) and is the only flagged operator. That's a conversation worth having.

**The one thing today:** Understand why Wednesday placed orders collapsed. Check whether there was an app or coverage issue. If operations were normal, then the problem is at the front of the order pipeline — not enough new people finding Tumbil, and the regulars took a day off. The 7-day cost per new customer at $34.48 is already $9.48 over the $25 ceiling; if Wednesday's pattern repeats, the economics break fast.

days.analyst_brief:
**The cost-per-new-customer problem is structural, not daily noise.** Friday's $34.99 per new customer is actually the best single day in the trailing week — the 7-day average is $40.14. Over the full 7 days, Tumbil spent $722.48 on ads and acquired 18 new customers. To get that 7-day average down to the $25 ceiling, you'd need roughly 29 new customers on the same spend — 60% more than you're getting. The spend itself is steady (~$103/day), so this is a conversion problem, not a budget problem. The ads are generating a consistent ~70 clicks per day; only about 2-3 of those turn into first-time orders.

The reorder cohort data adds some comfort here: recent cohorts are converting at 28-41% within 21 days, so the customers you do acquire tend to stick. But that doesn't fix the front of the pipeline. The April 13 cohort (41.2% reorder rate) is the high-water mark in recent months — if the quality of new customers is staying that high, the real lever is getting more of them through the door at a reasonable cost.

**Zero second-order customers on Friday is worth flagging.** The 7-day average is 2.0 per day, and there have been two other zero days this week (May 17 and May 20). That's 3 out of 7 days with no one coming back for a second order. This could be random variation on small numbers, but it's also the segment that determines whether your acquisition spend pays off — a new customer who never returns is a $35 loss. Worth watching over the next week to see if the second-order rate recovers.

**The cancellation rate keeps creeping up.** The rolling 7-day rate is now 5.5%, up 0.3 percentage points from yesterday's reading of 5.2%. The 30-day baseline is 3.1%. Five cancellations in the last 7 days vs 12 in the prior 30 — the recent week is running at nearly double the monthly pace. None of yesterday's 11 orders were cancelled, so the elevated rate is from earlier in the week. Still, this is tracking in the wrong direction and worth investigating — are specific cities, WashPros, or order types driving it?

**WashPro quality alert: WashPro 2323 has 3 low ratings in the last 30 days, averaging 3.0 stars.** Friday also saw a new 3-star rating for WashPro 2506 (order 5645). With overall ratings dipping to 4.71 yesterday vs the 4.80 7d average (1 low rating out of 14), service quality is holding broadly but these individual WashPros need attention. WashPro 2323 in particular — three low ratings is a pattern, not a bad day.

**On organic search:** all 42 monitored pages are indexed, no flags. The Kitchener city page stands out — 371 impressions over the last 7 days with zero clicks, averaging position 7.8. That's Google showing the page frequently but no one finding it worth clicking. Since the city page meta title and description are template-level (shared across all 24 city pages), a template-level title/description tweak would affect all cities — but the Kitchener data suggests the current template isn't compelling enough when the city name alone isn't a draw. Hamilton has a similar pattern: 420 impressions, 4 clicks, position 12.8. These two pages alone represent nearly 800 impressions per week with almost no traffic.

**The one thing today:** The acquisition cost problem won't fix itself at $103/day spend producing 2.6 new customers. Before adding budget or changing bids, it's worth auditing which campaigns and search terms actually produce first-time orders (not just registrations — the Google Ads conversion count of 7 yesterday includes registrations, not purchases). Friday's search terms show spend on "calgary laundromat" ($4.30, 0 conversions), "cleaning soiled clothes" ($2.51, 0 conversions), and other terms unlikely to produce pickup-and-delivery customers. The Calgary campaign spent $13.65 with zero conversions. Understanding where the 3 actual new customers came from — and whether some campaigns are burning budget on the wrong audience — is the highest-leverage 30-minute exercise.
